title: OSPF Neighbor Adjacency
description: The process by which OSPF routers discover each other, exchange database information, and synchronize their LSDBs.
fullExplanation: OSPF routers form adjacencies to exchange routing information. The process starts with Hello packets — routers discover neighbors and verify parameters match (area ID, hello/dead intervals, authentication, subnet mask). On multi-access networks (Ethernet), a DR (Designated Router) and BDR (Backup DR) are elected to reduce the number of adjacencies. Full adjacency means both routers have identical LSDBs.

neighborStates.description: Hello received but own Router ID not in neighbor's Hello
Bidirectional communication established. DR/BDR election happens here.
DBD packets exchanged — summary of LSDB

drBdrElection.description: On multi-access networks, DR and BDR are elected to reduce adjacencies from O(n²) to O(n)
electionCriteria: Highest OSPF priority (default 1, range 0-255). Priority 0 = never become DR/BDR.
Tie-break: highest Router ID (highest loopback IP, or highest interface IP)
drBdrElection.benefit: Without DR: n(n-1)/2 adjacencies. With DR: n adjacencies (all form full adjacency with DR/BDR only)

keyFacts: Dead interval: 4 × hello interval — neighbor declared dead if no Hello received
Parameters must match: area ID, hello/dead intervals, authentication, subnet mask, MTU
Router ID: highest loopback IP, or highest active interface IP if no loopback
DR/BDR election is non-preemptive — existing DR keeps role even if better router joins
Point-to-point links: no DR/BDR election — both routers go directly to Full state
